skill.name: azure-kubernetes
skill.description: Plan and configure Azure Kubernetes Service clusters with guidance for SKU choice, networking, identity, observability, upgrades, and cost controls.
skill.summary: Design production-ready Azure Kubernetes Service clusters with practical Day-0 and Day-1 guidance.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell-command and weak-cryptography patterns, but review found the command hits are documented Azure CLI and kubectl examples for legitimate AKS administration. The weak-cryptography alerts are false positives from ordinary words and AKS terminology, with no evidence of cryptographic code, malware, data exfiltration, or prompt-injection instructions that target the evaluator.

medium_findings.title: Infrastructure-Changing Command Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends Azure MCP tools and Azure CLI commands that can create, inspect, configure, stop, or modify AKS resources. This is legitimate for an AKS planning skill, but execution can change cloud infrastructure and should require explicit user confirmation, correct subscription context, and least-privilege Azure access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain explicit AKS provisioning and addon commands. They are appropriate for the skill purpose, but the operational impact is real if a user executes them against a live subscription.

low_findings.title: Benign Local Cloud Context Inspection
low_findings.description: The skill tells users to verify Azure login and subscription context with Azure CLI when MCP calls fail. This is a normal diagnostic step, and the instruction explicitly says not to echo subscription identifiers back to the user.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines reference standard Azure CLI context and cluster-listing operations. I found no evidence that command output is sent to an external party or used for unauthorized reconnaissance.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Cryptography Alerts Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak-cryptography detections occur in prose and AKS guidance, not in cryptographic implementation code. No evidence found of MD5, DES, RC4, SHA-1, hardcoded keys, or custom cryptographic routines in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows these locations are descriptive AKS planning text or command names. There is no executable cryptographic code in SKILL.md or references/cli-reference.md.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URL
low_findings.description: The skill links to Microsoft Learn documentation for Azure CNI Overlay. This network indicator is a user-facing reference link, not an automatic network request or exfiltration endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL points to public Microsoft documentation and is not used by code. No file contains logic that automatically fetches or transmits data to that URL.

dangerous_patterns.title: Azure CLI Commands Require User Approval
dangerous_patterns.description: The CLI examples include commands that create clusters, get credentials, and enable monitoring. They should be presented for review and not executed automatically by an assistant without direct user consent.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit infrastructure operations. The risk depends on execution context, so this is a publishable operational warning rather than evidence of malicious intent.

content.value_statement: AKS cluster planning requires early choices that are hard to change later. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through secure Azure Kubernetes Service design and setup decisions.
actual_capabilities: Helps choose between AKS Automatic and AKS Standard based on control and operations needs.
Guides Day-0 networking decisions, including API server access, pod IP model, ingress, and egress.
Recommends AKS identity and security controls such as Microsoft Entra ID, Workload Identity, and Azure Policy.
Outlines observability options using Container Insights, Managed Prometheus, Grafana, and diagnostic settings.
Provides upgrade, patching, reliability, and cost-control guidance for production AKS clusters.
Includes Azure CLI reference commands for common AKS planning and provisioning tasks.
limitations: It does not replace Azure architecture review for regulated or large enterprise environments.
It cannot validate subscription permissions, quotas, regions, or live cluster state without connected Azure tools.
It provides guidance and command examples, but users must review commands before running them.
It does not deploy workloads or write Kubernetes manifests for application teams.

use_cases.title: Plan a New Production AKS Cluster
use_cases.description: Create a structured recommendation for SKU, networking, identity, observability, upgrades, and cost controls before provisioning begins.

use_cases.title: Review AKS Networking Choices
use_cases.description: Compare Azure CNI Overlay, VNet-routable pods, ingress options, and egress controls before locking in Day-0 decisions.

use_cases.title: Prepare Secure AKS Operations
use_cases.description: Define identity, policy, monitoring, upgrade, and reliability practices for teams that will operate AKS clusters.

prompt_templates.title: Start an AKS Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a new AKS cluster for a dev/test environment. Ask only for the required inputs and suggest safe defaults.

prompt_templates.title: Choose AKS SKU and Networking
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare AKS Automatic and AKS Standard for my workload. Include Azure CNI Overlay, API server access, ingress, egress, and Day-0 tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Production Readiness Checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a production readiness checklist for AKS covering identity, secrets, policy, observability, upgrades, node pools, and reliability.

prompt_templates.title: Review an AKS Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this AKS design for security, reliability, cost, and operational risk. Identify hard-to-change decisions and safer alternatives.

output_examples.input: We need an AKS cluster for a production web application with predictable outbound IPs.
output_examples.output: A recommended design that covers AKS SKU, Azure CNI Overlay, restricted egress through Azure Firewall, Workload Identity, monitoring, maintenance windows, and cost controls.

output_examples.input: Our team is unsure whether pods must be routable from the VNet.
output_examples.output: A networking comparison that explains Azure CNI Overlay versus VNet-routable pods, with questions to confirm connectivity and IP planning needs.

output_examples.input: Prepare our AKS upgrade strategy for several clusters.
output_examples.output: A staged upgrade plan with maintenance windows, workload safeguards, node OS patching, Fleet Manager considerations, and rollback checks.

best_practices: Confirm subscription, region, quota, identity, and networking constraints before recommending provisioning commands.
Separate Day-0 decisions from Day-1 features so users understand what is hard to change later.
Require explicit user review before running Azure CLI commands that create or modify resources.
anti_patterns: Do not assume AKS Automatic is valid when the user needs unsupported custom networking or node pool controls.
Do not request, print, or store Azure tokens, kubeconfig secrets, or application credentials.
Do not promise zero downtime for upgrades without workload safeguards and staged validation.

faq.question: What does this skill help me design?
faq.answer: It helps design AKS cluster configuration, including SKU, networking, identity, security, monitoring, upgrades, reliability, and cost controls.

faq.question: Can it create an AKS cluster for me?
faq.answer: It includes Azure CLI examples and MCP guidance, but users should review and approve any command before execution.

faq.question: Does it support AKS Automatic?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends AKS Automatic by default unless the workload needs custom controls that require AKS Standard.

faq.question: Can it help with private networking?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers API server access, Azure CNI Overlay, VNet-routable pods, egress control, ingress choices, and DNS considerations.

faq.question: Does it handle secrets management?
faq.answer: It recommends Microsoft Entra ID, Workload Identity, and Azure Key Vault through the Secrets Store CSI Driver.

faq.question: Is it safe for production planning?
faq.answer: Yes, with review. It is guidance-focused, but commands can affect live Azure resources and should require explicit approval.
